metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` (`materialize_tickets`, `_has_origin`, `_fetch_origin`, `resolve_ref`) to AC3's edit-target list, since these functions hardcode `'origin'` when fetching/pinning the ticket store snapshot for the code-review gate.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py contains a ticket-sync network site (materialize_tickets()) that hardcodes 'origin' for fetching and pinning the shared ticket store — it is not listed in the plan's edit targets.
findings.location: AC3 edit-target list vs. src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py lines 607-622

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC5's test description to require coverage for each listed file/site in AC3 (push.py presence-guard + push/fetch/ref, sync.py reconverge + _do_reconverge, fsck.py _push_pending, init.py _mount_or_create_branch + pending_init_attaches_to_existing), OR add a proving command (e.g. `grep -rn '"origin"' src/rebar/` returns zero matches) so AC3 completeness is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies testing 'a network site uses the configured remote' (singular), but AC3 claims EVERY listed hardcoded site (push.py, sync.py, fsck.py, init.py — multiple call sites) uses the resolved remote. The single-site test described in AC5 does not prove the completeness claim of AC3; the remaining sites could still carry hardcoded 'origin' and all tests would still pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'Tests cover at least one call site per file listed in AC3; a `grep -rn \'"origin"\' src/rebar/` (or equivalent) step is run and returns zero results to confirm no residual hardcoded strings remain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6: the 'CLAUDE.md git-workflow section' does not currently exist — specify whether this section needs to be CREATED (and give a rough outline: e.g., 'remote layout: origin=GitHub tickets+mirror, gerrit=Gerrit code review; sync.remote config') rather than updated.
findings.finding: AC6 references a 'CLAUDE.md git-workflow section' as an existing edit target, but CLAUDE.md contains no such section — there is no 'origin', 'gerrit', or 'git workflow' content in that file at all. The plan would need to CREATE this section, not update it.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword AC6 to: 'Add a new "git-workflow / remote layout" section to CLAUDE.md documenting the split-residency setup (origin=GitHub tickets+mirror, gerrit=Gerrit code review) and note that `sync.remote` controls which remote the ticket sync targets; also add the `sync.remote` key to `docs/config.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) that the async push child in `push.py` correctly inherits the resolved `sync.remote` in all cases — specifically when `sync.remote` is set via CLI override (`-c sync.remote=X`), ensure `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE` is injected into `child_env` alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH`, mirroring how `tickets_branch` handles the `tracker.branch` override in the async path.
findings.finding: The async push child in push.py (lines 119-141) explicitly sets child_env = {**os.environ, 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH': 'always'} but does not ensure REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE is forwarded. If sync.remote is resolved from a config file (not the env) in the parent process, the spawned child inherits os.environ (which won't include REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE), and will resolve the remote name via its own load_config — which should work. However, the plan's description of the async path as part of push.py says 'push/fetch/ref' sites must use the resolved remote, but does not clarify that the async child independently re-resolves config and therefore does not need special env forwarding.
findings.suggested_fix: In the async dispatch block, add `child_env['REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE'] = resolved_remote` alongside the existing `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` injection, where `resolved_remote` is the result of the new `tickets_remote()` call resolved in the parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm in the plan (or a code comment in AC1's implementation) that `sync.remote` (`REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE`, a git remote *name*) is distinct from the review bot's `tickets_remote` / `TICKETS_REMOTE` (a full clone URL in `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py`) and that neither collides with nor supersedes the other.
findings.finding: The codebase already has a `tickets_remote` field + `TICKETS_REMOTE` env in `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py` (a full HTTPS clone URL for the Gerrit bot), and the plan introduces `sync.remote` + `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE` (a local git remote *name* like 'origin'). While these are genuinely different concepts, the naming proximity risks developer confusion and the plan does not acknowledge the existing symbol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the existing `tickets_remote` field in `review_bot/config.py` (a full HTTPS URL) when naming `config.tickets_remote()`; consider a disambiguating name (e.g., `tickets_remote_name()` or `sync_remote()`) or add a docstring cross-reference to prevent semantic confusion.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `config.tickets_remote()` helper (AC2), but `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py` already defines a `tickets_remote` field (line 85) on `ReceiverConfig` — a completely different object, holding a full HTTPS clone URL (not a git remote shortname). The naming is confusable: implementers and future readers of the codebase will encounter two `tickets_remote` symbols with opposite semantics (URL vs remote-alias) in the same project.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which coercer is used for `sync.remote` in `_SECTIONS["sync"]` — either document that `_as_str` with a non-empty guard is sufficient (remote names need no slash-component validation) or add a minimal `_as_remote_name` that rejects empty/whitespace and control characters, matching `git check-ref-format --allow-onelevel` semantics rather than branch-component rules.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `_SECTIONS["sync"]` will coerce `sync.remote` but does not name which coercer to use, leaving open whether `_as_git_ref` (branch-name rules), `_as_str` (no validation), or a new `_as_remote_name` validator should be applied. `_as_git_ref` is the only existing ref-validating coercer but it enforces branch-component rules (e.g., it rejects a leading `.` or `@{` sequences) that do not fully match git remote-name validity (`git check-ref-format --allow-onelevel`). Using the wrong coercer could silently reject valid remote names or accept invalid ones.
findings.location: AC line 1 — '`SyncConfig` gains `remote: str = "origin"` and `_SECTIONS["sync"]` coerces it in `src/rebar/config.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a lightweight `_as_remote_name(v, key)` coercer that: (1) calls `_as_str(v, key)`, (2) strips whitespace and rejects empty, (3) rejects control characters and spaces — mirroring what git itself enforces for remote names — and register it as `"remote": lambda v, k: _as_remote_name(v, k)` in `_SECTIONS["sync"]`. Do NOT reuse `_as_git_ref` (branch-component rules are stricter and incompatible with valid remote names like `my.host`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docs-update bullet to the 'What' section (e.g. 'Update CLAUDE.md git-workflow section and docs/config.md to document the new `sync.remote` key and the split-residency setup') so AC6 maps to explicitly described work.
findings.finding: AC6 (docs update for CLAUDE.md and docs/config.md) describes a concrete deliverable — two files to be edited with specific content — but the 'What' section does not mention documentation updates at all, leaving AC6 as a criterion covering undescribed work.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the 'What' section: 'Update project docs: add `sync.remote` to `docs/config.md` and revise the git-workflow section of `CLAUDE.md` to reflect the split-residency remote layout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test in AC5 that sets `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE` to a non-default value, resolves config (populating the cache), then changes the env var and re-resolves, verifying a fresh value is returned (not the stale cache hit) — exercising the cache-signature participation stated in AC1.
findings.finding: AC1 explicitly requires `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE` to participate in the config-cache signature, but AC5's test list does not include a test that verifies the cache is invalidated (or a new cache key is computed) when this env var changes. A stale cached remote name would silently use the wrong remote across process boundaries.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1 ('participates in the config-cache signature') and AC5 (test list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'cache is invalidated when REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE changes between calls (config-cache signature includes the remote key)'. This can be a unit test that calls `tickets_remote()` twice with different env values and asserts the second call returns the new value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which coercer validates `sync.remote` in `_SECTIONS["sync"]` and verify it accepts all valid `git remote` short-names (including dotted names like `gerrit.internal`); add or name a dedicated `_as_remote_name` validator if `_as_git_ref` would reject them.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_SECTIONS["sync"]` will coerce `sync.remote` via the 'same generic mechanism' but does not specify which coercer. The only named validator in the codebase for string-typed config keys is `_as_git_ref` (branch-name rules) and `_as_str` (plain string). `_as_git_ref` would reject valid git remote names containing dots (e.g., `gerrit.internal`, `review.corp`) because its per-component check rejects names starting with `.`; there is no existing `_as_remote_name` validator in `src/rebar/config.py`. Using the wrong coercer would silently break the Gerrit split-residency use-case the plan was written to enable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `_as_remote_name(v, key)` validator that rejects empty strings and chars illegal in git remote names (whitespace, control chars) but does NOT apply branch-ref path-component rules; use it in `_SECTIONS["sync"]["remote"]`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
